_note: Evergreen World Cup fact cards for trivia rails, sidebar modules, and between-section education. Facts with current-tournament numbers should be reviewed after the 2026 final.

facts.title: The first World Cup was played in 1930
facts.fact: The men's World Cup began in 1930 and has been staged every four years except during World War II.
facts.whyItPlays: It gives new fans a clean historical anchor right away.

facts.title: 2026 is the first World Cup hosted by three countries
facts.fact: The 2026 tournament is the first World Cup to be co-hosted by three nations: Canada, Mexico, and the United States.
facts.whyItPlays: It instantly explains why the tournament feels so spread out and city-driven.

facts.title: This is the first 48-team World Cup
facts.fact: The 2026 World Cup is the first edition with 48 teams instead of 32.
facts.whyItPlays: It helps explain why there are more groups, more matches, and more host-city storylines.

facts.title: The 2026 tournament has 104 matches
facts.fact: The new format uses 12 groups of four, sends the top two teams plus the eight best third-place finishers into a Round of 32, and expands the tournament to 104 matches.
facts.whyItPlays: This turns the format itself into a fun-size explainer card.

facts.title: Brazil owns the most men's World Cup titles
facts.fact: Brazil has won the men's World Cup five times, more than any other nation.
facts.whyItPlays: It is one of the fastest ways to explain why Brazil carries a permanent mythic aura.

facts.title: Brazil is the only team to appear in every World Cup
facts.fact: As of 2026, Brazil is still the only nation to have appeared in every men's World Cup.
facts.whyItPlays: It is a stronger brag than titles alone because it speaks to permanence and standard.

facts.title: Miroslav Klose still owns the goals record
facts.fact: Germany's Miroslav Klose remains the men's World Cup all-time top scorer with 16 goals.
facts.whyItPlays: It gives even casual fans a single all-time scoring benchmark to remember.

facts.title: One player scored 13 goals in a single World Cup
facts.fact: France's Just Fontaine scored 13 goals at the 1958 World Cup, still the record for one edition.
facts.whyItPlays: Even people who know modern stars are usually shocked by how enormous that number is.

facts.title: The biggest World Cup crowd was 173,850
facts.fact: The best-attended World Cup match on record is Uruguay vs Brazil at the Maracana in 1950, officially listed at 173,850.
facts.whyItPlays: It makes modern stadium atmospheres feel part of a much older emotional scale.

facts.title: Cristiano Ronaldo is the only player to score in five editions
facts.fact: Cristiano Ronaldo is the only men's player to score in five different World Cup tournaments.
facts.whyItPlays: It says as much about durability and era-spanning relevance as it does about goals.

facts.title: Pele was still a teenager when he made history
facts.fact: Pele remains the youngest World Cup goalscorer, scoring at 17 years and 239 days in 1958.
facts.whyItPlays: It is a perfect reminder that some World Cup legends arrive before the world is ready for them.

facts.title: The tournament is watched on a giant global scale
facts.fact: Wikipedia's summary of FIFA audience reporting says the 2018 World Cup reached an estimated 3.57 billion viewers and the 2022 tournament drove roughly 5 billion engagements.
facts.whyItPlays: It helps explain why even casual fans feel the World Cup in a way they do not feel most other events.

facts.title: Mexico becomes the first country to host World Cup games three times
facts.fact: With 1970, 1986, and 2026, Mexico becomes the first country to host or co-host men's World Cup matches three times.
facts.whyItPlays: It is a great host-story fact that feels local and historical at the same time.
